title: Popular History of Ireland by Thomas McGee Synopsis

enrichment.summary: Kyle gives an informal 35-minute rundown of Thomas McGee's 2,114-page 'Popular History of Ireland,' walking through Irish history from ancient Celtic kingdoms through St. Patrick's Christian era, the Viking invasions, Norman and British rule, and the catastrophic Great Famine of 1845–49. He mixes the book's content with his own general historical knowledge and personal genealogical interest in Irish-Scottish ancestry.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: very long and often boring, especially the Norman period; early books on Celtic and Viking Ireland are the most interesting
essentially colonial exploitation — absentee landlordism, discriminatory laws, and second-class citizenship as bad as American racism against Black people
England's mismanagement is to blame; food exports continued while millions starved, cutting the population in half
Ireland arguably saved Christianity after the fall of Rome by maintaining the strongest faith while England was overrun by pagan Germanic tribes
the 12,000 Irish sent to the West Indies and Virginia were slaves, not merely indentured servants as Wikipedia claims
brutal and culturally disruptive but also improved Irish seafaring technology
keeping synopses raw and informal encourages actually doing them and honestly representing what you know
